content:
Hi! I had my first Rust-for-Linux patch accepted recently, and I’m looking for the next issue to work on.

I noticed that issue #1215 has a patch sent last week based on a suggestion from @Alice Ryhl, and I wasn’t sure about its current status.

Is this issue considered taken for now, or would it be helpful for someone else to help with testing / review / follow-ups?

Just looking for guidance so I don’t duplicate work
The patch was merged today.
I think we still need to shrink the vector if it's much larger than number of processes.
Probably we need a new method on our Vec which moves all elements from one Vec to another.

Thanks for the clarification!
I’ll take a look at what a Vec toVec move/shrink helper could look like and see if I can propose something.

I noticed that issue #1215 has a patch sent last week based on a suggestion from @Alice Ryhl, and I wasn’t sure about its current status.

You can search at https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/ as "Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1215"
One tip/suggestion: when you send a patch for an issue, please add a link back to lore.kernel.org in the GitHub issue. That way it is easy to check in both directions. :)

Miguel Ojeda said:

One tip/suggestion: when you send a patch for an issue, please add a link back to lore.kernel.org in the GitHub issue. That way it is easy to check in both directions. :)

Updated
Thanks for the tip.
Thanks :)

Is this issue considered taken for now, or would it be helpful for someone else to help with testing / review / follow-ups?

By the way, on this: more reviews and testing always helps, so please feel free to do that and to reply with your Reviewed-by and Tested-by tags etc.

Sometimes that helps to move patches forward, because maintainers see increased confidence on a series.

Onur Özkan said:

You can search at https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/ as "Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1215"

This is what I see, on the top a patch by Jason Hall. Next thing I am trying to figure out is if a patch is accepted and merged.
firefox_4nVKm8Qvqb.png

If you look at the git repository at git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/char-misc.git, you will find the commit applied to char-misc-next. That means it will land in the next Linux release.
Greg, the maintainer of char-misc, sent an email to the people involved when he applied it, but I don't think you can see it on the list.

Greg, the maintainer of char-misc, sent an email to the people involved when he applied it, but I don't think you can see it on the list.

Oh, I see. I have been playing around with lore.kernel.org search today, trying to learn it. Will look at this git repo later today.

Alice Ryhl said:

Probably we need a new method on our Vec which moves all elements from one Vec to another.

This might be a basic question about workflow for a follow-up patch in this situation, what would be the right base to work from?

Should it be based on char-misc-next, or is there another tree you’d prefer for Rust-side changes like this?
In this case, char-misc-next would make sense.
Got it, thanks! I’ll base it on char-misc-next

Hi @Alice Ryhl
I'm looking at the KVVec shrinking logic. Since deregister_process returns (), I assume the shrinking operation should be "best-effort" (i.e. if the allocation for the smaller buffer fails, we just keep the existing capacity and return success)?
Also, do you have a preference for the API?

  1. A standard shrink_to_fit() -> Result<()> (caller handles errors/ignoring).
  2. Or a helper like condense() that handles the heuristic internally (e.g. only shrink if len < cap / 2)?
Yes it should be best-effort.
I think a standard shrink_to() method is fine.
Thanks! I will work on implementing shrink_to for KVVec

Hi! I'm working on the follow-up patch based on char-misc-next.

When I run make LLVM=1 CLIPPY=1 drivers/android/, I'm hitting a clippy::needless-borrow error in drivers/android/binder/context.rs:142 (introduced by the recent KVVec refactor).

It seems innocuous (just an extra &), but it breaks the build when CLIPPY=1 is set.

Should I include a fix for this as the first patch in my series, or would you prefer I ignore it for now?
Please do send a fix, and you can even do so now, prior to the rest of your series.
One more thing, do we create driver issues on Github? Or I explain the issue in the email?
You should just explain the issue in the email (you can paste the entire warning into the commit message).
Got it.
I only create issues on github when I think someone new to the kernel might want to work on the issue.
I guess I am no longer new :)

do we create driver issues on Github?

It depends -- subsystems (and drivers etc.) usually have their own procedures and/or issue trackers (see MAINTAINERS file). But sometimes, for Rust code, if it is something not really tied to a particular file but that may require changes there, we may use our "global" tracker. For instance, a cleanup on a core API that affects many callers, or a change in the global guidelines, etc.

For instance, Nova and Tyr (and DRM in general) have their own trackers. And Alice mentions here for Binder she only uses it for good first issues. And so on. Some maintainers just track everything via email.

In summary, you should check the MAINTAINERS file to try to see where it is best to report something. For a report, in many cases the best is just a quick email to the maintainers/lists of the relevant files (unless it is a security bug). And if you have a patch already, then sending the patch is most likely enough (i.e. again, an email :).

I hope that helps.

In summary, you should check the MAINTAINERS file to try to see where it is best to report something. For a report, in many cases the best is just a quick email to the maintainers/lists of the relevant files (unless it is a security bug). And if you have a patch already, then sending the patch is most likely enough (i.e. again, an email :).

I understand. Different maintainers have different preferences. No one size fits all.

Alice Ryhl

Please do send a fix, and you can even do so now, prior to the rest of your series.

Link
shivamklr has marked this topic as resolved.
